title: Making Kosher Chinese Food: A Complete Guide

sources.title: Homemade Chinese Food
sources.author: Elizabeth Kurtz
key_points: Kids love Chinese food - make it at home
Save money, nutritious meals
Recipes: Wonton Soup, Asian Garlic Ginger Chicken, Chicken Lettuce Wraps, Sesame Chicken
Mirin available at kosher markets
Leftovers reheat well

sources.title: Where Exotic and Kosher Meet
sources.author: Shifrah Devorah Witt and Zipporah Malka Heller
key_points: Asian food elegant enough for Shabbos
Sushi with fish and vegetables - cost-effective
Cookbook covers Chinese, Japanese, Thai, Filipino, Indian
Recipes simplified for home cooks
Teriyaki salmon, sushi rice, dipping sauces

sources.title: Easy & Delicious Chinese Chicken and Vegetables
sources.author: Miriam Szokovski
key_points: Fabulously easy recipe
Chicken velvety soft and flavorful
Prepare in morning, bake 30 minutes before dinner
American-style Chinese, less labor-intensive
Note: All processed foods must bear kosher certification
Leafy greens must be checked for infestations
All cooking on kosher utensils, meat and dairy separate

sources.title: Chinese-Style Chicken Pot Sticker Dumplings
sources.author: Miriam Szokovski
key_points: Pot stickers are The Perfect Food
Crispy bottom, soft tender top, juicy flavorful inside
Time-consuming but not difficult
Save for leisurely cooking occasion
Includes homemade dough and filling

sources.title: Chinese Decide to Eat Jewish Food on Their Holidays
key_points: Historical connection: 1910 NYC Lower East Side
Jews and Chinese largest non-Christian immigrant groups
Chinese restaurants open Sundays/holidays
Jewish law prohibits mixing dairy and meat
No problem in Chinese restaurants - dairy rarely used
Kosher restaurants employed Chinese chefs
Cultural exchange between cuisines

sources.title: What Is Kosher?
key_points: Hebrew 'kosher' (כָּשֵׁר) means 'fit'
Torah-mandated guide for what and how Jews eat
Only kosher meat allowed
Meat and dairy must be kept separate

sources.title: Meat, Dairy and Pareve - The Kosher Kitchen
key_points: Three categories: meat, dairy, pareve
Basic principle: total separation of meat and dairy
Pareve foods can be eaten with either

chinese_parallels.parallel: Just as Confucian tradition emphasizes proper conduct (礼 lǐ) in all aspects of life including dining, kosher laws bring mindfulness to eating
Similar to traditional Chinese medicine's hot/cold food balance (热性/凉性), kosher laws maintain careful separation of meat and dairy
Chinese cuisine historically adapted to local ingredients along Silk Road; Jewish diaspora adapted kosher laws to new environments while maintaining core principles
Torah dietary laws given at Mount Sinai (~1300 BCE) during China's Shang Dynasty (商朝, ~1600-1046 BCE), when both cultures developing foundational religious/philosophical texts
